data.title: Understanding Master Limited Partnership Payout Ratios
data.description:
In a previous article,[1] we explored how payout ratios for real estate investment trusts (REITs) are often misstated by public financial data platforms. These platforms typically calculate payout ratios as dividends divided by net income, which can give a distorted view of a REIT’s true distribution capacity.

That’s because REITs use a different capital structure, where depreciation and other non-cash items heavily impact net income. A more accurate picture comes from comparing distributions to adjusted funds from operations (AFFO), which accounts for the recurring cash flow available to shareholders.

The same challenge applies to another pass-through, income-focused structure backed by real assets: the master limited partnership (MLP). While the accounting terminology differs from REITs, many of the same pitfalls arise when evaluating MLP payout ratios using standard net income figures.

In today's article, we’ll use Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), one of the largest and most widely held MLPs, as a case study to show what is the key input to watch when assessing distribution sustainability.

Understanding EPD's true payout ratio

As of July 9, Yahoo Finance shows a payout ratio of 78.65% for Enterprise Products Partners.[2] That figure on its own isn’t necessarily a red flag. After all, EPD is an income-focused MLP that prioritizes returning capital to investors.

But the number is misleading because it’s calculated using net income, not the cash EPD actually generates and uses to fund its distributions.

For MLPs like EPD, the better metric is distributable cash flow, or what the company calls Adjusted Cash Flow from Operations (Adjusted CFFO).

This is a non-GAAP measure that reflects the net cash provided by operations before accounting for the timing of certain working capital changes. In EPD’s words, it strips out fluctuations tied to when the company receives or pays cash, giving a more accurate picture of how much cash its assets are consistently throwing off.

The rationale is straightforward. A company’s ability to sustain or grow distributions depends on steady, repeatable cash generation, not short-term swings in receivables or payables. So instead of focusing on net income or even GAAP operating cash flow, EPD prefers to present Adjusted CFFO as a clearer view of how much it can reinvest or return to unitholders.[3]

For the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, EPD reported $8.6 billion in Adjusted CFFO. When you compare this to not just its cash distributions but also its buybacks (EPD includes both in its payout calculation), the picture looks much more conservative.

The company’s April 2025 earnings release puts the total payout ratio at just 56%, a far cry from the 78.65% shown on Yahoo, even accounting for changes in the ensuing months.

That’s why relying on public payout ratio figures without digging into a company’s reporting can lead to a skewed assessment, especially with MLPs that operate under different accounting assumptions and prioritize different forms of capital return.

Not all MLPs will use the same language

Adjusted CFFO may be the metric of choice for EPD, but it’s far from a standardized term across the MLP industry.

Head over to EPD’s major competitor, Energy Transfer L.P. (ET), and you’ll see a different approach entirely. In its first quarter 2025 earnings report, the phrase “Adjusted CFFO” doesn’t appear at all.

Instead, ET uses Distributable Cash Flow (DCF), another non-GAAP measure built on the same principle. It reflects the cash available to return to unitholders after removing non-cash earnings and deducting capital needed to maintain operations.[4]

Specifically, ET defines DCF as "net income, adjusted for certain non-cash items, less distributions to preferred unitholders and maintenance capital expenditures."

What this shows is that payout metrics can differ not only in terminology but also in how they’re constructed. In addition, some MLP management teams will clearly disclose a payout ratio as a percentage of DCF, but others won’t.

In ET's Q1 2025 report, for instance, there’s no mention of a payout ratio at all; you’d need to calculate it yourself using the reported DCF and distribution figures.

Remember, public platforms often default to net income-based metrics that don’t reflect how these companies operate. If you want a more accurate picture of their financial sustainability, the only way to get there is to read the earnings reports closely.

Management will usually define their preferred metrics, outline the inputs, and explain how they arrived at them, but only if you know where to look.

Footnotes

[1] Understanding REIT Payout Ratios

[2] EPD Yahoo Finance

[3] Enterprise Reports First Quarter 2025 Earnings

[4] Energy Transfer Reports First Quarter 2025 Results

data.title: One Of The Biggest Investment Opportunities Wall Street Overlooks
data.description:
Introduction

On July 9, I wrote an article titled "Water You Waiting For? One Of My Favorite Ideas I've Ever Written About." As always, that title was 0% clickbait, as the water business of the Permian Basin has become my favorite place to put money, as I believe investors, in general, have no idea how big of a bottleneck the water issue has become in the (global) energy sector.

As bullish as I am on the prices of oil and gas on a longer-term basis, I think it's nearly impossible to beat the attractiveness of the water business, as oil and gas producers are starting to "pay anything" to get rid of the toxic water byproduct that is increasingly making it impossible to maintain production.

Ever since I wrote that article, I have been more obsessed than ever by the way bottlenecks have major impacts on global supply chains and how this can shape investment opportunities.

One of these bottlenecks is developing in one of the world's biggest asset classes: gold. Initially, I got the idea from Horizon Kinetics legend Murray Stahl, who brought it up in an investor roundtable on June 26.

Since then, I decided to fully dive into it and build my own thesis, as it seems Wall Street is overlooking what could be a very unusual bull market for many years to come, with a lot of opportunities for income and growth-focused investors.

So, let's keep this intro short and get right to it!

Bullish On Gold, But For A Different Reason

Gold has been doing absolutely fantastic lately, trading close to $3,500 per troy ounce, roughly $1,000 more compared to the prior-year level.

As I have written in prior articles, this is due to a number of reasons:

  • The dollar has weakened quite substantially this year.
  • The U.S. government seems to be unable to reduce the deficit.
  • Inflation remains an issue.
  • Financial conditions have eased.
  • It seems the government may be picking growth over fighting inflation.

While I believe all of these factors are bullish, they are all macro factors.

Investors are overlooking something critical, which is on a supply-chain level.

This is the part where it becomes really interesting.

See, while inflation is a factor pressuring gold miners, they are doing just fine. Although gold and silver mining stocks have underperformed the price of gold on a long-term basis, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) is up 55% year-to-date, making it one of the best years in modern history.

This makes sense, as miners are "printing money" right now.

For example, Newmont Corp. (NEM), one of the largest miners on the planet, has a 2025E AISC of $1,620. AISC stands for "all-in sustaining costs," meaning it costs barely more than $1,620 to produce an ounce of gold.

In the case of Newmont, these costs consist of labor (50%), materials (30%), and fuel and energy (15%). If you have ever watched the famous Gold Rush series on Discovery, you probably know just how tough this business can be.

With that said, if you net almost $1,400 per troy ounce of gold (painting with a broad brush), one can assume that even the most conservative gold miners are considering boosting production. Even if higher supply growth could hurt the price a bit, with margins this big, the incremental gain can be huge.

Right?

Well, that's not happening.

Newmont, for example, aims to produce 5,600 thousand ounces this year, down from 5,680 thousand ounces in 2024.

Needless to say, I am cherry picking here. However, while there are miners who are boosting output, the industry, in general, is not.

In fact, gold production has been flat since 2018, as we can see below. Since then, the price of gold has risen by nearly 160%. If this were the case in the oil industry, producers would spend every single minute of every day figuring out how to extract as much oil from the ground as possible.

So, why is that?

There are a number of reasons for this, and they could come with tremendous investment opportunities. As I noted in the intro, that's why I am writing this article.

  • Geological constraints and the decline of quality

For starters, ever since industrial gold mining started to result in serious global output, producers have been looking for the highest-grade deposits. That's obvious, as these reserves come with the lowest operating costs.

After many decades of elevated production growth, miners have been running out of the best reserves, similar to what oil and gas producers are facing in the United States.

Two years ago, S&P Global focused on this issue, noting that:

Copper and gold head grades have fallen over the last decade. The average copper head grade in 2022 was 0.52% Cu, and the average gold grade was 1.31 g/t Au, down 7.5% and 13.4%, respectively, since 2012. The average reserve grade over the same period has a similar story. The average global copper reserve grade has dropped 9.1% from 0.54% Cu in 2012 to 0.50% Cu in 2022, while the average gold reserve grade has remained flat at 1.26 g/t Au since 2012. As shown in the chart below, gold mines have been mining at a higher grade than the reserve grade for much of the last decade, but the difference between the mined and reserve grade has started to close since 2020. - S&P Global

This means miners need to mine an increasing amount of material just to keep production flat, as the gold share of mined materials is steadily falling.

In other words, it requires a lot more labor, energy, materials, and planning to maintain production, regardless of the price of gold.

  • Soaring production costs

While mining margins are very high, miners are well aware of the risks facing their OpEx and CapEx (operating expenses and capital expenditures).

For example, gold mining is getting a lot more energy-intensive, as extracting one ton of gold can require up to 20,000 megawatt-hours of energy. Especially deep-level underground mines are becoming expensive, as these require a lot of ventilation and cooling, which often account for nearly half of a mine's total energy consumption. In an environment of exploding power demand from data centers and other drivers, energy is becoming an increasing issue.

Then there's water, as the industry faces increasingly strict environmental regulations regarding water contamination from byproducts like cyanide and mercury that are used in the extraction process.

Just like in oil, water is extremely critical in the gold and silver mining (or mining in general) process, which means lower ore grades, stricter environmental regulations, and scarcer resources will likely be a huge problem for anyone looking to grow (or even maintain) current production rates.

In general, inflation is becoming an issue, as less productive areas are already seeing average costs of roughly $3,000 per ounce.

South Africa's gold sector recorded the largest increase in input prices in March 2025, with average costs reaching $2,986 per ounce (approximately R1,754,316 per kilogram). This trend highlights the growing pressure on producers, particularly those dealing with deep-level, low-grade ore bodies where extraction is inherently more expensive. - Discovery Alert

Then there's another factor.

  • Mining needs people

While mining is increasingly autonomous, including autonomous dump trucks, humans remain critical in this industry.

MinersAI noted that younger people aren't interested in mining anymore. Two years ago, the Mining Industry Human Resources Council found that only a third of Canadians aged 15-30 would consider a career in mining, making it the least attractive sector in Canada, as we can see below.

To make matters worse, half of the U.S. mining workforce is expected to retire by 2029, with a steep decline in new graduates.

Similarly, a McKinsey & Co article highlighted that the mining sector is facing a significant decline in mining engineering graduates, with US numbers decreasing 39% from 2016-2020. This generational shift is occurring at a time when half of the U.S. mining workforce is expected to retire by 2029, according to the National Mining Association. The gap between retiring workers and new entrants poses a serious threat to meeting production demands and supporting the transition to renewable energy. - MinersAI

  • Geopolitical risks

One of the reasons why I never really liked investing in gold miners is geopolitical risks. Owning mines across the globe comes with a lot of risks, including local political risks, wars, and whatnot. Kinross Gold (KGC), for example, sold its Russian assets after the invasion of Ukraine a few years ago.

Personally, my opinion has always been that the higher the price of commodities, the higher the political risks, as foreign nations (in, i.e., Africa) may suddenly oppose foreign nations exploiting their high-cost reserves, even if they are benefiting from it through labor, royalties, taxes, and other factors.

My opinion is getting confirmed by scientific papers:

Nations safeguard their financial stability and meet external debt obligations by maintaining a diverse portfolio of reserves. These reserves encompass natural resources, like gold, oil, and metals, as well as financial assets, including foreign currency reserves [...]. - Ngo et al. (2024)

Moreover:

Our study reveals significant impacts of geopolitical risks, as measured by the Geopolitical Risk (GPR) index and major geopolitical events, on countries' reserves and resource management strategies. The results indicate that higher geopolitical risks, as reflected by the GPR index, negatively impact countries' total reserves relative to external debt. This suggests that high levels of geopolitical risks create challenges for countries in accumulating resources as reserves relative to the increase in external debt. - Ngo et al. (2024)

As China, Russia, and various African and South American nations lead the list of the largest global gold producers, it's easy to see that geopolitical risks are increasing due to deglobalization. This shift emphasizes the importance of "allied" production giants like Australia, Canada, and the United States for low-risk operations.

So, what does this mean for asset allocation?

Actionable Ideas (Food For Thought)

The gold thesis, in general, is far from hidden. After all, the metal has more than doubled over the past five years. The same goes for gold miners.

As I explained in this article, my thesis isn't just that the current environment is favorable for gold, but that gold production could even DECLINE in the years ahead, creating a lasting tailwind that could propel the world's most famous metal to much higher highs.

However, it's important to be picky, which explains why I am still not making the case that the GDX ETF (or any similar ETF) is the way to go. While I expect it to move higher, I think there are better places to generate alpha.

The miners I prefer are Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Kinross Gold.

Agnico Eagle Mines is a company I started covering in 2023. The Canadian company operates 11 mines in four nations. All of these are safe jurisdictions allowing the company to produce roughly 3,500 thousand ounces of gold last year.

Moreover, it has an AISC margin of 59% as of 1Q25, a number that's 17 points above 1Q24 levels. The total AISC was a mere $1,183 in the first quarter, a blowout number that I expect the miner to beat its peers for many years to come. It also has close to zero in net debt.

Because of its healthy balance sheet, I expect a substantial part of its capital allocation to be re-allocated to buybacks and dividends.

Currently, AEM yields 1.4%.

Then there's Kinross, the company that sold its Russian assets after the Ukraine invasion. Currently, it owns six mines that allow it to produce roughly 2 million ounces per year with reserves of 22 million ounces (excluding new findings).

Now, it exclusively produces in North America, South America, and Africa, with more than 50% of its production coming from Tasiast and Paracatu. Even better, 2/3rd of its production has a cost of sales of less than $1,000 per ounce, which explains why it is a leader in key metrics like free cash flow yield.

In fact, both Agnico and Kinross stand out when it comes to per-unit free cash flow generation. Also, at this point, it may be unbelievable, but I did not use the chart below to arrive at my top-2 miners.

Unfortunately, KGC yields just 0.8%, which means it's not a great investment for income. But that's OK, as both Kinross Gold and Agnico Eagle have "crushed" the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) over the past ten years.

Two other stocks I have to bring up are Franco-Nevada Corp. (FNV) and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM). These companies, which yield 0.9% and 0.7%, respectively, do not mine gold. Instead, they finance miners by providing loans. In exchange, they receive royalties and streams.

This means they get to buy a portion of the production below the spot price, allowing them to benefit from gold and silver production without having to take on CapEx and OpEx risks. Or to put it differently, it's nearly impossible to beat the margin profiles of these companies.

The major difference between Franco-Nevada and Wheaton is that Franco-Nevada also ventured into oil and gas royalties, an area I am quite bullish on as well.

While both have done well, I expect them to continue their uptrend in the years ahead, benefiting from a lot of production growth (without any additional capital requirements), healthy balance sheets, and extremely favorable industry fundamentals.

And, needless to say, physical gold also benefits.

Whether you have an ETF like the Gold ETF (GLD) or a few ounces at home or at the bank, I think it's a better investment than the S&P 500.

Takeaway

The gold bull run isn't just about macro tailwinds. It's about a pending supply crisis no one is talking about. At least not yet.

Despite all the good news, flat gold production since 2018 tells the real story, which is that collapsing ore grades, soaring energy and labor costs, and geopolitical risks are pressuring output growth.

In other words, miners aren't chasing higher prices because they can't.

That's why I'm doubling down on Agnico Eagle and Kinross Gold, which are low-cost producers in safe jurisdictions with massive margin benefits.

And for those who want gold exposure without mining risks, royalty giants Franco-Nevada and Wheaton remain fantastic companies.

Last but not least, physical gold remains a hedge worth holding.

All things considered, the bottom line is that I believe the gold supply situation is massively underestimated, potentially causing a prolonged uptrend benefiting a wide range of companies beyond the ones I presented.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is short-term headwinds from falling gold prices, potentially triggered by a strengthening dollar, a steep decline in inflation, and/or substantially higher supply growth.

I believe the odds of all of these things are extremely low.

data.title: 8%-Yielding Portfolio: Building The Near-Perfect Dividend Snowball
data.description:
The search for the near-perfect dividend snowball is a quest that I devote much of my time to. Why do I do this? Because a dividend snowball (i.e., an ever-growing stream of passive income from dividends that grows faster and faster, the larger the portfolio gets, similar to how a snowball gets larger and larger and picks up more and more snow with each revolution as it rolls down the hill and gets larger and larger) is a phenomenal way to build long-term wealth. This is because you have numerous factors working in your favor, all of which are putting the power of compounding to work for you.

First of all, dividend growth stocks increase their dividend per share each year. Additionally, your number of shares grows each year as you reinvest the dividends that you earn into buying new shares. Finally, as the dividends per share grow consistently year after year, this is generally indicative of growing intrinsic value per share for the business as well, which in turn tends to push the stock price higher and higher. Therefore, you get dividend growth from both more shares and higher dividends per share, and you also get greater principal from both capital appreciation per share as well as from owning more shares and reinvested dividends.

Perhaps even better than this is the fact that focusing on growing your dividend income stream completely inverts your mindset about market performance. This is because, as a dividend investor, you want the stock market to go down so you can buy more shares than you would have otherwise and get a higher starting yield on those shares. Meanwhile, if the stock market goes up, it tends to disappoint dividend investors because this means that they can only buy fewer shares and at lower starting yields with their income. Ultimately, all that matters is that your dividend income grows over time rather than getting short-term gains in the stock market. Besides, as long as dividend income continues to grow, it is inevitable that the market value of the portfolio will eventually increase as well.

However, building the optimum portfolio requires proper diversification and balance. This is because if you concentrate too much in a single sector, you expose yourselves to major risks, whether they be disruptions from technology or a black swan event such as the COVID-19 outbreak, which led to mass lockdowns and very negatively impacted various businesses, including even blue-chip dividend machines like Simon Property Group (SPG), which had to slash its dividend in the face of lockdowns of its Class A retail properties, and Energy Transfer (ET), which had substantial cash flow coverage of its distribution and investment-grade balance sheet at the time, but was rattled by the crash in energy prices and the need to protect its investment grade credit rating, leading to a 50% distribution cut.

Additionally, beyond simply diversifying across sectors, it is also important to have a healthy balance between more aggressive stocks and very conservative stocks. Granted, if you are in retirement with a substantial nest egg, you may be focused purely on very sustainable dividend income and sustaining your principal. You can lean heavily on the more conservative stocks. In fact, you may simply just want to buy an ETF like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and call it a day.

However, if you are earlier on in your investing journey, as I am, and implementing an opportunistic capital recycling strategy overlaid on top of your dividend snowball in order to accelerate the compounding process of both your passive income and your total returns, a broader balance is many times better. This is because the more aggressive stocks will generally give you a higher yield and potentially greater capital appreciation potential. Meanwhile, also maintaining adequate exposure to some lower-risk stocks helps to minimize some of the risks and can also set you up for stronger performance during periods where the market becomes more risk-averse.

With these principles for building a near-perfect dividend snowball in view, in the rest of this article, I am going to lay out a sample portfolio that I view as reflective of a healthy balance of diversification across sectors as well as diversification across more aggressive and more conservative holdings within each of those sectors.

Building The Dividend Snowball Portfolio

My favorite sector to find dividend streams in is the midstream sector. In fact, most of these holdings pay what are considered to be distributions rather than dividends, including the three I am going to mention here. On the riskier end of the spectrum, Delek Logistics Partners (DKL) does not have an investment-grade balance sheet and is fairly small in size, which therefore makes it a bit riskier, especially given its outsized exposure to its main counterparty in Delek US Holdings (DK). That said, it does pay out a 10% distribution yield that is well covered, and it has a long streak of growing its distribution every quarter with plans to continue doing so for the foreseeable future, making it a highly compelling income machine. In addition, with its highly contracted cash flows as an infrastructure business, the 10% yield should be quite sustainable for the foreseeable future.

In the middle of the risk spectrum is Western Midstream Partners (WES), which pays a 9% distribution yield that is backed by an investment-grade balance sheet and has very low commodity price exposure with long-term contracted cash flows. However, it has heavy exposure to Occidental Petroleum (OXY), who also own a lot of units of WES. OXY has been known to aggressively sell off some of its units in the past and could do so again in the future, which could put significant pressure on the unit price.

Finally, on the conservative end of the spectrum, you can buy blue-chip C-corporations and K-1 issuers like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB), The Williams Companies (WMB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), or TC Energy (TRP). All of these offer yields of up to 7% and can be great ways to anchor your portfolio with low risk. Alternatively, you can simply buy an ETF like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), which pays out a very attractive yield of nearly 8% while having broad diversification across the sector, including a heavy concentration in some of midstream's leading blue chips.

In the REITs sector, you can go out and buy well-covered 8% yields like those I recently detailed here, including the likes of Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH), Easterly Government Properties (DEA), and even a 6.7% dividend yield that has a strong dividend growth momentum and a BBB+ credit rating like Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE). On the more conservative end of the spectrum, you can get nearer 6% yields from the likes of NNN REIT (NNN) and Realty Income (O), or you can increase your yield a bit by buying an actively managed CEF like the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) that offers a 7.8% dividend yield with monthly payouts. It is broadly diversified and actively managed with a track record of beating the broader REIT sector (VNQ) over time.

In the BDC space (BIZD), I recommend steering clear of anything too risky, given the fact that these funds already tend to invest in fairly risky individual loans in their portfolios and leverage them up to a significant extent. Additionally, management quality matters a lot, though you have to be careful of overpaying for these sorts of BDCs. One of my favorite BDCs right now is the Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), as it offers an over 10% yield that is fully covered by earnings. It has a very defensive portfolio that is almost entirely made up of first-lien senior secured loans, has one of the lowest management fees of any externally managed BDC out there, has very low non-accruals, and a strong balance sheet. In addition, it trades at a discount to its net asset value. There are other peers that are blue chips that are also exceptional income machines with impressive long-term track records, like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Main Street Capital (MAIN), and Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC). However, you will pay a premium for each of those today in the market, so you have to decide how much buying on a value basis matters to you.

Finally, you can round out the portfolio and get broader diversification across sectors by investing in some quality ETFs and CEFs. One ETF that many investors like because of its 9.4% dividend yield is the Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA). Granted, it does employ some leverage, which enhances its volatility but also enhances its yield, making its monthly payments quite attractive and a way to further diversify to include more of a fixed income component. It is also worth noting that this fund is actively managed, and it implements a capital recycling program within it in order to seek to maximize total returns and continue to grow the payout over time. While it had to cut its dividend during the 2020 crash, since then it has been able to grow its dividend, and since launching, it has handily outperformed the broader preferred sector (PFF).

You can also further diversify into infrastructure with some closed-end funds like the Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG) and the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), both of which have monthly distribution yields of around 7% and track records of sustaining and even growing their payouts over time while also delivering long-term total return performance due to skilled management teams. These funds can also boost your portfolio by giving you some exposure to the AI boom, as many of their individual holdings are positioned to benefit as important infrastructure players in the accelerating AI boom.

Following the AI theme, you can also gain exposure to the mega-cap tech space while generating a very attractive yield through funds like the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) or even the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI). These also pay monthly distributions that are generated through monthly call options that are sold against the underlying Nasdaq 100 index. The one big negative of these funds is that they are at risk of long-term NAV erosion due to the fact that the calls cap upside during rallies while underlying holdings still participate fully in downturns. An example of this is seen with the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), which has a similar underlying exposure as JEPQ and QQQI, though its covered call strategy is a bit different. Nonetheless, it has been around long enough to show how this strategy can potentially cause long-term harm if not implemented very carefully.

Therefore, if an investor does not need the income, I suggest pursuing exposure to the mega cap tech space outside of the income portfolio and simply holding a diversified Nasdaq or growth fund like the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), or even an S&P 500 index fund like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). That said, if you feel like you need the income from those funds to meet your income objectives, there can be a place for a small allocation to a fund like JEPQ or QQQI in your portfolio. Finally, you can round out the portfolio if you need further diversification with a fund like SCHD due to its broad diversification across numerous sectors, over 100 blue-chip dividend stock holdings, a fairly attractive dividend yield of nearly 4%, and an impressive near-11% dividend per share CAGR over the past decade.

You could put these names together to get a really high yield if needed. For example:

Security Yield Weighting
DKL 10.0% 5%
WES 9.0% 5%
AMLP 7.9% 10%
AHH 7.9% 5%
DEA 7.9% 5%
RQI 7.8% 10%
MSDL 10.2% 5%
BXSL 9.5% 5%
PFFA 9.4% 15%
UTF 7.1% 12.5%
QQQI 13.9% 10%
SCHD 3.8% 12.5%
Weighted Average 8.0% 100%

That being said, keep in mind that this portfolio needs to be actively managed and carefully monitored in order to mitigate risks. Additionally, regular opportunistic capital recycling can be used to accelerate the compounding process of both the yield and the total returns, as many of these holdings are unlikely to grow that much beyond the dividends they pay and potential capital appreciation. Also, keep in mind that this is just a sampling of ideas for investors seeking to maximize current yield while still pursuing diversification and some modest overall dividend growth. It is not necessarily a suggestion that each of these investments is meaningfully undervalued at present.

Investor Takeaway

Building the best possible dividend snowball portfolio is easier said than done, but if accomplished, you can unlock a lifetime of financial freedom, which in turn can give you the time and material resources necessary to maximize your contributions in other even more important areas of life like family, philanthropy, and even active participation in worthy causes like serving God and other causes you are passionate about. This is what motivates my efforts to maintain an effective balance between risk and reward in the dividend positions that I buy, as well as being disciplined in maintaining optimal diversification as I put portfolios together at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Tariffs And Inflation Expectations
data.description:
Here are some things I think I am thinking about this weekend:

The Teeny Tiny Tariffs

Back in April, I laid out 3 likely scenarios for how the tariffs would play out. The most likely scenario I laid out, at 70% odds, was one where Trump had to backpedal on most of the tariffs and the economy basically reverted back to where it was before, but with minor damage. The key to this was the deal with China because we had a near trade embargo in place until that happened. Thankfully, it was short-lived. I’d say my scenario 2 has more or less played out, and the best evidence of this is that there has been very little meaningful change from the actual tariff implementations so far.

It’s interesting because, despite all the constant noise about tariffs, we’re seeing very little actual economic impact. This is clearly seen in actual tariff tax receipts. For example, through the first 6 months of this year, we’ve incurred $86B in total customs duties. In 2024, we incurred $82B. So we’re on track to more than double customs duties. But $86B just isn’t that much in the grand scheme of things. For example, back in April, when the tariff banter was at its peak, the administration was saying they could replace the income tax with tariffs. We’re talking about a $3 trillion line item there, so markets panicked because that would have been a colossal tax increase. But we’re nowhere close to that occurring, and the math never made sense to begin with because you can’t collapse global trade and also expect global trade to generate trillions in revenue. For reference, we’ve incurred $1.5T in individual income tax receipts through the first 6 months of the year. $86B might sound like a lot of money, but for an entity that accrues $5T of annual receipts, $86B just doesn’t move the needle that much. You’re talking about 1-2% of total tax receipts.

So, I think that’s a big part of why markets have surged back, inflation remains tame, and the economy hasn’t slowed significantly. Despite all the bigly talk about tariffs, they’ve turned out to be teeny tiny tariffs so far. That could change in the coming year if the administration gets more aggressive implementing them, but for now, this has turned out to be a much smaller actual policy lever than it was presented as in April.

Mean Reversion in Inflation Expectations

We had a huge surge in inflation expectations when the tariffs were first announced, but there has been very little flow through from the tariffs to actual price changes. That’s mostly due to the prior point – corporations haven’t had to boost prices materially because they haven’t incurred significant actual tariff costs. And so now we’re seeing bigly mean reversion in inflation expectations.

On Friday, we got the U Michigan Consumer Sentiment data and it shows a big decline in inflation expectations (chart via Liz Ann Sonders, Schwab’s wonderful Chief Strategist). I suspect this is going to collapse right back to where it was before long. I never thought the tariffs would be inflationary to begin with, but that median reading got wildly out of whack in my view.

In short, it’s been a weird 6 months of 2025. In some ways, it feels like nothing has happened, and in other ways, it feels like everything has happened. I don’t know what to make of it. All I know is that all this market volatility resulted in me doing a whole lot of leg days, and so my cycling FTP has exploded higher and my max squat is at an all-time high (I have a lame recurring joke on Twitter where you have to do leg day, when the market is down 1% or more). I used to joke about my theory of “assflation” (that’s asset price inflation if you’re new around here), but now it’s coming back to haunt me as my glutes grow. For some reason, the beer belly doesn’t go away despite all this exercise. Inflation, man. You just can’t defeat it, no matter how hard you try.

I hope you’re having a great weekend, and as always, stay disciplined.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ManpowerGroup: Stabilizing, But Not Yet A Buy
data.description:
Introduction: Where I Stand After Q2

The general sentiment around ManpowerGroup Inc. (NYSE:MAN) over the last year or so has been decidedly bearish. On Seeking Alpha alone, I’ve seen 7 articles published in that period. 3 are Sell/Strong Sell, and the rest are Hold, with only 1 Buy position, and that came in nearly a year ago. The stock is down 40% in the same period, so I suppose it all tracks: investors look at market sentiment and the company’s trajectory and outlook, and markets follow investor commentary too, so it’s a bit of a vicious cycle.

Now, the company’s Q2 2025 results are in, and generally speaking, we should see a tiny bit of positive sentiment going off the numbers. Still, even though there aren’t any major surprises, I think the overall outlook is one of caution.

Still, the macro picture hasn’t changed much. Employers are cautious, client orders are choppy, and headcount adjustments continue to weigh on volumes. Structurally, the business is solid, and at these levels, a lot of bad news is already priced in, but I don’t see enough momentum yet to justify anything besides a Hold.

Company Overview

ManpowerGroup is a pretty simple company if we break down what it’s about. What it does is pretty straightforward: connect people with jobs. Most of its revenue comes from temporary and permanent staffing through its Manpower segment. Then we have Experis (IT and professional resourcing), Talent Solutions (RPO, MSP, and career transitions), and Right Management (leadership and outplacement services).

This is a global business with operations in 80+ countries, but Europe pulls the most weight, especially France. The country alone accounts for over 20% of MAN's total revenue. Needless to say, that kind of exposure comes with a lot of baggage: weak economies, tight labor laws, and fluctuating hiring cycles.

Now, management has been talking up their higher-margin solutions for years, and there’s real potential there. But the core business is still based on staffing, and when hiring slows down, as it has lately, we can see how quickly it hits the numbers.

I’m going to review Q2’s results below.

The Setup: Q2 2025 in Focus

Consolidated Revenue & Margins

Revenue came in at $4.5 billion, down about 3% year-over-year, and the weakness is most visible in Europe. The company’s EBITA (operating profit minus amortization & goodwill impairment) took a hit too, down 34%, and that reflects a tough combo of slower top-line growth, higher SG&A, and limited pricing power in a sluggish labor market. However, on the plus side, gross margins held relatively steady at 16.9%, so it’s not a total wash. If we go further down the P&L, we land at a GAAP EPS of -$1.44, dragged down by $88.7 million in goodwill impairments tied to Switzerland and the UK, plus some minor hits from the sale of South Africa and New Caledonia. Just strip that noise out, and we get an adjusted EPS of $0.78, which is above the company’s midpoint of guidance.

Regional Trends

The story has a bit more nuance when you break it down by geography.

  • Americas (23% of revenue): Revenue here was flat, but if adjusted for constant currency (CC), it rose 2%. OUP fell 20% (18% CC) to $36 million, with margin dropping 80 bps to 3.4%. The region is stable, but that stability is hiding some softness, especially in professional staffing and Experis demand. We can see that with the big decline from Canada, specifically.

  • Southern Europe (47% of revenue): Revenue rose 2% in real terms but fell 3% CC, while adjusted OUP dropped 16% to $75 million, with margin narrowing to 3.5%. This is MAN’s most dominant region in terms of revenue size and contribution, but we’re seeing it continue to be squeezed by macro uncertainty and competition.

  • Northern Europe (18% of revenue): Still a mess. Revenue dropped 10% CC and OUP margin slid into negative territory at -0.8%. I think the UK and Switzerland impairment charges point to structural issues here, and the performance is weighing on the entire company's results.

  • APME (12% of revenue): This was the bright spot. Revenue fell 8% CC, but operating profit margin improved 50bps to 5.1% as adjusted. The region is leaner, more disciplined, and generally more stable. Permanent hiring held up well, and we can see that the demand in places like Australia and Japan showed more life than their Western counterparts.

Now, I want to examine each business line’s performance.

Business Line Highlights

ManpowerGroup’s three core business lines told different stories.

  • Manpower Staffing saw 2% revenue growth (1% CC), and that marks a return to growth after several quarters of contraction. I’d say that the rebound is a positive sign, especially given the line’s heavy exposure to general staffing in a weak labor market.

  • Talent Solutions rose 3% reported and 1% CC. RPO trends were slightly better than Q1, and MSP continued its upward momentum. Right management was the main laggard here, and most of the pressure came from lower outplacement demand.

  • Experis is still the weakest link. Revenue fell 7% as reported and 9% in CC, dragged by the non-recurrence of healthcare tech projects in the U.S. It’s a tough reset for what was once a high-growth engine.

All things considered, then, this was a mixed quarter: far from a disaster, but it’s certainly not enough to flip sentiment just yet. I'd say that the headline numbers show that there's some stability. Still, I can't discount the margin erosion, regional underperformance (especially in Northern Europe), and sluggish demand in Experis because they all point to a setup that isn’t riding the challenges too well.

What I'm Watching in Q3 and Beyond

1. Experis Reset: Can It Stabilize?

Experis used to be Manpower’s growth engine, especially during the post-COVID IT boom, but that’s not the case these days. Q2 saw a 9% CC revenue decline, and we still don’t have real visibility into when the company will start seeing a rebound in demand for specialized tech staffing. I’ll be watching whether Experis can hold the line in Q3 or if the softness continues, because without Experis pulling its weight, there isn’t much of a near-term growth narrative.

2. Northern Europe: Structural Drag or Just a Rough Patch?

That -0.8% margin in Northern Europe is hard to ignore. Between the impairments in Switzerland and the U.K., ongoing macro pressure, and tight client budgets, this region is a real drag on MAN’s consolidated results. Management said they’re focused on fixing this via restructuring the business, and we can see some other signs of potential positive momentum. Still, any fix won’t happen fast. In Q3, I’m watching for how revenue stabilizes and whether the region can get back to breakeven margins.

3. Cash Flow & Cost Discipline

Additionally, it looks like Manpower’s cost control efforts are starting to pay off. SG&A as a percentage of gross profit improved slightly in Q2, and adjusted EBITA margin held steady despite the drag from Northern Europe. Q3 may be softer in terms of real numbers, but I’ll be watching free cash flow trends and how tightly they manage costs to protect profitability.

Valuation Check: The Market’s Not Wrong

Manpower’s stock has dropped about 40% over the past year, and it’s trading at $44.36 per share. That puts it at a forward P/E of roughly 18.2x, based on consensus estimates of about $2.95 earnings per share for FY 2025. The EV/EBITDA multiple is around 8x, and its dividend yield is about 3.34%, which is fairly attractive, but it’s partly a reflection of the depressed share price.

I’m interpreting all of those things, combined with the macroeconomic factors and the business’s performance in Q2 to mean that:

  • The market isn’t missing anything. Most investors are pricing ManpowerGroup as a cyclical, structurally challenged staffing provider.

  • That 18x earnings multiple suggests limited growth expectations, almost as if investors just want to wait it out.

  • An 8x EV/EBITDA is on the low end, even for staffing peers and outsourcing names (which tend to trade in the mid-teens).

Just looking at the multiples can be a tad misleading, though. After all, nearly everything is at a discount to MAN’s peers and its sector, so it’s likely that one might think it an undervalued opportunity right now.

The bottom line with the valuation as it is that there's little margin for error at these levels. The market’s clearly bearish, and it’s not unreasonable. If macro and staffing demand remain soft, then I think it’s more likely that the multiples will stay low or even fall further. Alternatively, if Q3 shows stabilization in Europe or signs of recovery in Experis, there’s a real shot investors could re-rate the stock. For now, though, it’s a Hold.

The Risks

  • Europe is fragile: Management says the softening in Southern and Northern Europe is stabilizing, but I’m not ready to take that as gospel. If the region gets any renewed macro weakness over the rest of the year, it could hit both revenue and margin pretty hard, and that tanks any positive sentiment Q2’s results may have built up.

  • Experis still has work to do: IT staffing is showing some signs of life, but what is more obvious is that the recovery is patchy. We need to see stable, consistent growth from this higher-margin segment; otherwise, that potential rebound will stay muted.

  • Valuation traps exist: Just because it’s cheap doesn’t mean it’s a bargain. I believe that if MAN’s EPS estimates fall or revenue keeps declining, then the stock may look deceptively “undervalued” for a long time.

Where I Stand Now

There’s a lot to like about how management is stabilizing things, especially with the margin beat and free cash flow this quarter. But the overall setup is still fragile, and the macro risks haven’t disappeared. The valuation isn’t demanding, but it’s not low enough to make me jump in, given all the moving parts. I’ll keep watching for consistent growth in Experis and margin recovery in Europe, but for now, I’m sticking with a Hold.

data.title: Gartner: Solid Fundamentals, Discounted Valuation
data.description:
I think Gartner (NYSE:IT) shares are attractive to buy right now. The company has a strong and sustainable model — annual research subscriptions provide stable cash flow and good margins. The securities are currently trading at a forward P/E of about 32x, which is 27% lower than the average over the past five years, despite the fact that both growth and business profile have remained at the same level. Active share buybacks help accelerate EPS growth, which is likely to support quotes. Altogether, a stable business, predictable FCF, and a reasonable valuation give me a reason to give a "Buy" rating. In this review, I will take a closer look at the key drivers and figures on which my thesis is based.

High-Retention Research Business Anchors Gartner's Cash Flows

Gartner, Inc. is a large publicly traded company providing analytical research, consulting services, and conferences in the field of information technology and business strategies. Most of Gartner's revenue (82% in 2024) comes from research subscriptions with a high proportion of renewals, while conferences and consulting account for 9-10% each and depend on one-time projects. Gartner operates globally: the company has over 2,500 analysts and 960 consultants in more than 30 countries, and products are sold in approximately 90 countries and territories.

Gartner's competitors include other large information services firms such as Forrester Research, IDC (International Data Corporation) and various independent consulting agencies, as well as analytics units in large IT companies (for example, IBM Consulting). In addition, open Internet resources and new AI services that partially meet the information needs of customers are becoming a competitive factor. Competition in the market remains high: according to the company, it faces many alternative sources of research and advice, and the barriers to entry are quite low.

The main demand for Gartner's services is based on the growth of IT budgets and business interest in new technologies like AI and the cloud - many simply cannot do without external expertise in these matters. The Gartner model positions the company favorably: thanks to long-term contracts and a high level of customer retention (multi-year contracts take up a large share), the company's revenue becomes fairly predictable. At the same time, the Conference business may vary (depending on the dates and format of events), but it is an important addition, especially as offline events normalize after the pandemic. As long as companies continue to invest in digital transformation and technology becomes more complex, interest in analytics and consulting is unlikely to weaken in the coming years. This creates the foundation for stable cash flow, which the market is still underestimating, especially against the background of adjustments in the tech consulting sector.

Q2 Set for In-Line Revenue, Mild EPS Upside on Buyback

In the first quarter of 2025, Gartner added 4.2% in revenue, which is $1.53 billion, and taking into account the currency, the growth is even higher, at 5.7%. All three segments have grown, but as before, subscriptions have made the main contribution: Research is once again pulling the main growth, while Conferences and Consulting are slowly recouping customers after a pause. Thus, the growth of Research sales and the delayed growth of the Conference provided sustainable operational results. Net profit remained at $210.9 million, while EPS increased to $2.71 (+1.5%), primarily due to buyback. In general, the profit was retained rather due to savings and careful handling of taxes.

Management has revised its expectations for the whole of 2025: revenue is now expected to be slightly lower — $6.535 billion against the previous $6.555 billion, but profitable metrics have been raised — Adjusted EBITDA is now $1.535 billion, and EPS is $11.70. The decrease in revenue is mainly due to the currency and current dynamics, while the profit growth is due to the same cost control, especially in the second half of the year, where the company promises to be flexible but careful with costs.

In the second quarter, I expect revenue to grow by about 5% year-on-year. Research will provide the main contribution again — customers continue to extend, retention is consistently high. In the Conference segment, demand is stabilizing and is likely to pick up due to the return of offline events and a seasonal revival. Consulting may add a little if partial project activity continues to recover. According to my estimates, the EBITDA margin will rise to 25.5%, primarily due to the scalability of the business and strict cost discipline. EPS is likely to grow slightly faster than profit: buyback continues to reduce the number of shares outstanding, so the range of $2.80–2.90 per quarter looks achievable. The consensus now expects EPS of $3.30 and revenue of $1.67 billion, with year-on-year growth of 2.6% and 5%, respectively. Overall, my expectations are consistent with the consensus, with a slight EPS upside due to buybacks and potential margin improvements.

Valuation Still Trails Fundamentals

I built the estimate on the DCF model, putting into it the unlevered FCF for 2025-2030. According to my calculations, Gartner's revenue will grow by an average of 9% per year, as I expect demand for analytics to recover, as well as the expansion of the company's product line during these years. According to my expectations, the operating margin will stabilize around 19% due to scalability and operational leverage. The capex will remain as low as ever and will be around 1.6–1.8% of revenue, while working capital will continue to steadily contribute about 2% to FCF. As a result, the company will steadily convert 20-21% of revenue into free cash flow.

I calculated the WACC at 9.03% (the prerequisites are listed in the table above).

If you look at the multiples, Gartner is currently trading at about 32x forward P/E and 18.3x EV/EBITDA. This is slightly higher than the average estimate for the IT sector - there are about 30.7x and 15.3x, respectively, but it is still noticeably lower than its own averages over the past five years: 44.3x and 22.9x. The same goes for Price/Sales — the current 4.3x level is higher than the sector average (3.1x), but inferior to the average of Gartner itself (4.8x). This arrangement shows that the market is still cautiously laying the potential, and future growth does not seem to be fully taken into account in the current price. If the company moves according to plan, there is a good chance that the multipliers will return closer to historical levels, and this will give the stock additional support.

In my DCF model, I take a forecast for free cash flow, calculate the WACC, and then go to the final estimate using the P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers for terminal value. According to the scenario with a 9% profit growth and a P/E in the range of 26x–30x, a fair price is obtained in the range of $406–$434 per share, which gives an upside of 12-20% to the current price of $386. With an EV/EBITDA of 18x–20x, the estimate is slightly lower — $396–$430, with an upside of 9.5–19%. Both approaches show that stocks still look undervalued. Based on these data, we can talk about the underestimation of stocks relative to historical and market benchmarks, especially in the case of plans for profit growth. Based on consensus on EV/EBITDA 18x and P/E 28x, as well as a 9% increase in profit, the target price is $420, which gives an upside of about 8.8%. I assign a Buy rating with a target price of $420 with a 10-15% growth potential.

EPS Support Through Consistent Share Repurchases

Gartner historically does not pay dividends and focuses on the buyback program: for example, in 2024 it repurchased shares worth $735 million, and in the first three months of 2025 – another $163 million. This policy actively supports the share price and increases EPS by reducing the number of shares outstanding. At the same time, the company states that the return of capital through buyback remains a priority, while maintaining financial flexibility: there are no significant debt repayments planned for the foreseeable future, and free cash flows will be directed primarily to price support.

Risks

The deterioration of macroeconomics (lower IT budgets of companies, geopolitical instability) may slow down the growth of contract sales. Increasing competition: In addition to large consulting and media firms, Gartner is faced with free online solutions and rapidly developing AI services that may eventually take away some of its customers. Finally, internal risks include technological backwardness (the need to release in-demand solutions in a timely manner, including with AI functions), the difficulty of maintaining a qualified team of analysts and consultants, as well as operational risks (for example, failures at conferences or changes in tax and regulatory conditions). Any of these factors can negatively affect stock prices.

Final Thoughts

Gartner remains a fundamentally stable company with a predictable revenue model and high quality cash flow. The market assessment still does not fully reflect the growth potential, which creates a window for reassessment in the case of the medium-term plan. Given the current multipliers, DCF valuation, and capital return structure, the stock looks undervalued.

data.title: Fresh Del Monte Produce Remains Appealing As Q2 2025 Earnings Near
data.description:
Back in the middle of January of this year, I decided to reaffirm Fresh Del Monte Produce (FDP) as a ‘buy’ candidate. Even though the firm had experienced weakness leading up to that point, I argued that shares were cheap enough that recent improvements were attractive enough to justify a bullish outlook. Since then, the stock has risen by about 8.9%, outperforming the 5.1% increase achieved by the S&P 500. This comes at a time when revenue continues to drop. However, profitability for the company has ticked up, and shares still remain cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms.

Because of this, I am choosing to remain bullish. However, it is important to keep in mind that conditions can change as new data comes out. According to management, before the market opens on July 30th, the company will be announcing financial results for the second quarter of its 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts do anticipate that revenue will increase. But unlike what we saw in the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, profitability is expected to pull back. So long as we don't see anything significantly outside of the norm in terms of volatility, I can't really see myself changing my mind. But if the data justifies it, I certainly will.

Shares Remain Appetizing

The most recent data that we have for Fresh Del Monte Produce as of this writing would be results covering the first quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. In the chart above, you can see revenue, profits, and cash flows, for this window of time compared to the same time last year. Revenue hasn't changed much. In fact, if you round it to the nearest $10 million figure, it remained virtually flat at $1.10 billion. This was in spite of the fact that the firm enjoyed a decent uptick from its largest segment, which would be its Fresh and Value-Added Products segment. Overall revenue grew from $676.8 million to $683.2 million.

That rise, according to the company, was thanks to higher per unit selling prices of avocados and higher sales volumes and per unit selling prices of fresh cut fruits in North America. Things would have been better had it not been for the fact that fresh cut vegetables and vegetables as a whole reported lower sales because of strategic operational reductions that the company implemented in the final quarter of last year. This included the sale of some of its assets from its Fresh Leaf Farms business.

The weakness for the company came from its Banana segment, which saw revenue contract from $379.5 million to $363.8 million. There was a slight drop from the Other Products and Services side of the company from $51.6 million to $51.4 million because of lower net sales of its third-party ocean freight services that it chalked up to weather related and port congestion impacts and the role that they played in reducing volumes. For the most part, it was the Banana segment that proved to be the problem. Based on the data provided, this was caused by lower sales volumes and per unit selling prices in Asia as demand decreased and as the industry experienced excess supply. Lower sales volumes in North America caused by industry supply and weather-related logistics disruptions also negatively affected results, as did certain foreign currency fluctuations.

Even though revenue for the company dipped ever so slightly, profitability actually improved. Net income went from $26.1 million to $31.1 million. This, according to management, was driven by a rise in the firm's gross profit margin from 7.43% to 8.39%, and it was also thanks to a reduction in selling, general, and administrative costs from 4.58% of sales to 4.38%. The picture here would have been even better had it not been for an extra $14 million gain on the disposal of property, plant, and equipment, that the company booked in the first quarter of 2024. So if anything, the improvements on the bottom line for the first quarter of this year were rather impressive.

For the gross profit side of things, it appears as though the company benefited from margin improvement associated with higher selling prices for pineapples and melons, as well as because of higher per unit selling prices in its poultry and meats business. Cash flows for the company improved also. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $18.7 million to $46.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $35.7 million to $46.3 million. Finally, EBITDA for the company grew from $43.5 million to $61.3 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years. Revenue declined, and bottom-line results were mixed. We do know that the big problem here ended up being its Banana segment, which reported a plunge in revenue from $1.64 billion to $1.48 billion. Management blamed this on competitive market pressures in North America, as well as decreased supply in the Philippines that negatively affected sales volumes in Asia. The latter of these seems to have been the result of weather-related events. Fortunately, the Fresh and Value-Added Products category for the company actually reported a nice jump in revenue from $2.48 billion to $2.61 billion. This was because of higher per unit selling prices and greater sales volumes in avocados and pineapples. Higher sales volumes of melons also helped.

Despite these mixed results, the fundamental picture for the company remains solid. The firm has net debt of only $204.5 million, which is small compared to its market capitalization of $1.59 billion and the fact that adjusted operating cash flow last year was $187.7 million. This suggests that the firm could pay off most, if not all, of its debt in a rather short window of time, even with factoring in capital expenditures like the $51.7 million it spent in 2024.

On top of this, the company remains attractively priced. Even if we ignore the recent increase in profits and cash flows that we saw in the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year, the stock remains cheap. In the chart above, you can see how it's priced using results from the last two completed fiscal years. These are low multiples in my book. Furthermore, they are also low compared to other similar companies, as shown in the table below. Using each of the two valuation metrics, we can see that only one of the five companies is cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Fresh Del Monte Produce 8.5 7.7
Limoneira (LMNR) 9.9 41.2
Village Farms International (VFF) 35.9 77.0
Mission Produce (AVO) 12.5 9.3
Calavo Growers (CVGW) 14.0 14.2
Dole plc (DOLE) 6.0 5.6

Of course, if management hits its targets for this year, things could look even better. Management is currently working on cutting costs and growing by means of acquisition and product innovation for the 2025 through 2027 fiscal years. In fact, the business recently purchased a Ugandan avocado producer by the name of Avolio and is targeting 2% sales growth this year as well as double digit gross margins for 2025. Considering that the gross profit margin for the company last year was 8.36%, that would be quite an increase.

Naturally, the picture can always change as new data becomes available. In the table below, you can see that analysts are anticipating revenue of $1.16 billion for the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year that management will be reporting the results of on July 30th before the market opens. That would represent an increase over the $1.14 billion reported the same time last year. However, earnings per share are forecast to decline from $1.12 to just $0.95, taking net income down from $53.6 million to $45.9 million. If profitability does decline, it's likely that the other cash flow metrics will also drop year over year. But so long as the picture ends up not being worse than last year on a forward basis, I think investors have reasons to be very optimistic.

Takeaway

At this time, I think that the market is underestimating Fresh Del Monte Produce. Yes, the firm has experienced some volatility in the last couple of years now. But the good news is that shares are cheap, debt is low, and management seems optimistic when it comes to 2025. When you add all of this together, I believe that it makes for an appetizing prospect as earnings near.

data.title: Credicorp: Fundamentals Are Shining But Valuation Leaves Little Room For Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Credicorp (BAP) is a leading bank in Peru, with its operations extending into other Latin American countries and the US. The company has been going through a cleanup phase marked by a consistent improvement in the asset quality ratios. While this cleanup phase led to slow loan growth for the last few quarters, the loan growth going forward is expected to improve as management focuses back on growth after the cleanup. The NIMs are also expected to remain stable with repricing crosswinds from assets and liabilities. While fundamentals look bright, the company’s valuations do not have good upside potential. This, when combined with the political risk in Peru, discourages me from issuing a buy rating on the stock despite good fundamentals. Hence, I have a neutral (hold) rating on the stock.

Fundamentals Are Strong And Improving

Back in May, BAP came out with a robust start of 2025, with the company reporting significant YoY improvement in both the top and bottom lines. The topline came in at 1.44 billion, 7.4% higher than the same period last year, primarily driven by better NII growth driven by lower funding costs as well as growth in the non-interest income. On the bottom line front, the company reported $484 million in net income, growing 20% YoY.

On a more granular level, the company’s net interest income grew by ~4.3% YoY, primarily driven by lower funding costs due to the repricing of liabilities on the back of lower rates as well as a mix shift toward lower-yielding liabilities. During the latest quarter, the company reported a staggering 17% growth in the low-cost deposit balance, leading to an increasing share of this low-cost deposit, which is also evident in the consistently declining cost of funding over the last few quarters. As far as the yields are concerned, a similar dynamic is at play, but here the assets are being repriced, leading to declining NIMs.

With respect to loan growth, the company reported somewhat muted growth of ~1.5% on a YoY basis. This growth rate is in continuation from the last few quarters, where the company constantly posted low single-digit to slightly negative growth rates. The slower-than-expected loan growth is primarily attributed to a deliberate attempt to improve asset quality and tighten lending standards. Lending institutions like BAP have two options: either grow slowly with stable asset quality or grow fast and run a risk of asset quality deterioration. The management of BAP chose to grow slowly at better asset quality, especially when their main market (Peru) has been struggling with economic woes recently in late 2023, which is now recovering. Moreover, the company did a phenomenal job, with the company reporting 16% YoY growth in deposits, continuing the growth rate from the past few quarters. While the growth in deposits was partially fueled by the BCP Bolivia’s accounting adjustment, even excluding this benefit, the deposit growth would have been a robust 9.7% YoY for 1Q25.

With respect to asset quality, BAP has shown quite a remarkable improvement over the last few quarters. The company reported a YoY improvement of 112 bps in nonperforming loans (NPL); on a QoQ basis, the improvement was 16 bps. If we look at the overall trend for the last few quarters, there has been a steady improvement in the NPL ratio as well as other asset quality metrics. Moreover, the leading asset quality indicator has also witnessed quite a remarkable improvement over the last few quarters, with the internal overdue loan [IOL] ratio declining consistently over the last few quarters, as highlighted in the table below.
